title: AI Works, Pull Requests Don't: How AI Is Breaking the SDLC and What To Do About It
gist: CircleCI Principal Engineer Michael Webster presents data showing that headless AI agents are now pushing code into GitHub repos at hundreds of thousands of events per week, creating a severe queuing bottleneck in human-gated PR review processes that erases velocity gains. He argues that the critical investment priority must shift from AI task/tool definitions to automated validation pipelines—test impact analysis, flaky test elimination, and CI-as-judge—as the only scalable path to capturing AI productivity benefits.

themes.theme: AI-driven PR volume crisis and queuing theory bottleneck

insights.claim: AI coding tools can produce 1,500 lines of code in 10-30 minutes, roughly six times more than a human reviewer can assess in one hour (estimated max ~500 lines/hour), creating a structural mismatch between AI output velocity and human review capacity.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: As AI coding adoption scales, PR review becomes the primary throughput constraint; engineering orgs must rearchitect review workflows or velocity gains will be systematically offset.
insights.quote: Somebody can produce 1,500 lines of code in probably 10 minutes with Claude. If you're lucky and they massage it, maybe in 30 minutes, so they're like six times what you can actually review as a human.

insights.claim: Median PR merge time across organizations ranges from 3 to 14 hours, meaning a 10-minute AI coding sprint can wait 3-14 hours for a merge, making the net delivery speed-up minimal.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: The business case for AI coding investment depends on compressing downstream delivery latency, not just generation speed; tools targeting PR acceleration have large addressable pain.
insights.quote: When you look across some research that some companies have done around median PR review time, you see it can range between 3 hours and 14 hours.

insights.claim: Queuing theory simulations show that a 75% AI-driven increase in code throughput—without corresponding delivery pipeline speedup—causes PR queue delays to approach infinity, wiping out all productivity gains.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Investing in AI coding tools without simultaneously upgrading CI/CD throughput is a losing trade; infrastructure and delivery vendors benefit disproportionately from the agentic transition.
insights.quote: If you get up to about a 75% increase in AI in throughput...The delays that you would see waiting for someone to look at your change would basically go to infinity.

insights.claim: The well-documented 'review inversion' phenomenon—where 5-line PRs attract 50 comments, 10-line PRs attract 5, and 500-line PRs get LGTM—is actively worsening as AI agents produce ever-larger pull requests.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Large AI-generated PRs are systematically less reviewed than small human PRs, meaning technical debt and bugs enter codebases largely unchallenged, creating risk exposure.
insights.quote: People have talked about the 5 lines get you 50 comments, 10 lines get you 5, 500 lines get you LGTM. This is a very real effect.

themes.theme: Headless AI agent adoption trajectory and GitHub data

insights.claim: GitHub Archive data shows that headless AI agents (Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, and similar) reached hundreds of thousands of GitHub activity events per week within weeks of their respective launches, with a notable shift from review/triage activity to push events beginning around May 2026.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: AI agents have crossed a threshold from review assistants to autonomous code committers; the infrastructure for managing agent-generated commits at scale is now a critical product category.
insights.quote: You have AIs that are pushing just as much code as they're writing.

insights.claim: CircleCI's internal build data mirrors the GitHub-wide trend—paid CI builds attributable to headless agents appeared 'out of nowhere,' confirming that enterprise customers are deploying agents in production delivery pipelines, not just experimenting.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: CI/CD platforms will see structural volume growth from agent workloads; this is a durable demand driver for compute and tooling vendors.
insights.quote: These are real customers applying this to real-world software projects.

insights.claim: The DORA report on AI-assisted software development found that while agents increase velocity, they simultaneously increase defect rates and change fail rates, confirming that speed gains come with stability costs.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Quality assurance, testing, and observability tooling vendors are positioned to capture spend as organizations try to claw back stability lost to agentic coding.
insights.quote: They also found an increase in instability, so higher defect rates, higher change fail rates.

insights.claim: A multi-month study of thousands of open-source projects using AI IDEs found only one month of increased velocity before returning to baseline, attributed to 'persistent technical debt accumulation' causally linked to reduced long-run velocity.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Short-term AI productivity spikes may mask medium-term slowdowns; investors should scrutinize AI-driven productivity claims that rely on trailing 30-day metrics.
insights.quote: "We observe persistent technical debt accumulation in the changes from the AI assistants." I think this is probably something that folks intuitively think or understand is happening, but this is a study that was showing this with a causal relationship to a reduction in velocity.

themes.theme: Validation-first architecture as the strategic priority

insights.claim: Webster argues the industry over-indexes on AI task and tool definitions while undervaluing the 'check' phase of the agentic loop; robust automated validation is the highest-leverage investment for capturing AI productivity gains.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Vendors offering automated test generation, coverage analysis, and CI validation tooling are strategically better positioned in the agentic era than pure coding assistant vendors.
insights.quote: I think a lot of times in the industry, we focus a lot on the task piece, and we talk a lot about the tools piece, both of which are important, but I think we undervalue the check piece.

insights.claim: Once an organization has a test suite where passing tests reliably indicate production readiness, all bottlenecks in the delivery pipeline—including PR review—can theoretically be eliminated, enabling fully autonomous continuous deployment.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Organizations racing to build comprehensive, trusted test suites will achieve sustainable AI velocity advantages over competitors still relying on human review gates.
insights.quote: Once you have a good validation, once you have a test set that says, if these tests pass, this code is good to go to production, you've basically eliminated all the barriers and the bottlenecks.

insights.claim: Webster frames the PR itself as architecturally mismatched to AI agent workflows—the core problem is not PR size but the asynchronous, serialized nature of human review that cannot scale linearly with AI generation rates.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Developer tooling that replaces or bypasses PR-gated workflows with continuous validation gates represents a structural opportunity in the SDLC toolchain.
insights.quote: There's no fundamental law of the universe that says that a PR review or a code review has to happen before the code is deployed.

themes.theme: Test impact analysis as throughput multiplier for AI agents

insights.claim: CircleCI implemented test impact analysis on its main UI monolith (7,500 tests, 10 parallel instances) and cut build time from ~4 minutes to 1.5 minutes on a single machine by pruning tests to only those covering changed code paths.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Test impact analysis combined with coverage-dependency graphs can dramatically compress CI feedback loops for agentic workflows, making it a key enabling technology for AI-native SDLC.
insights.quote: When we applied this test impact analysis approach to it, we were able to cut that entire build time with no parallelism on a single machine down to one and a half minutes.

insights.claim: Test impact analysis enables AI agents to run only the subset of tests relevant to their specific code changes—executing inside a single Docker container or laptop—eliminating the need for complex parallel test farm orchestration for agentic builds.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Coverage-guided test selection is an enabling technology that makes AI agent CI runs economically tractable; tooling vendors that integrate this will see strong adoption.
insights.quote: Instead of having to figure out how do we parallelize and farm out all of these agents and how do we parallelize all their test runs...we can just make it so that it's tractable to run them inside of a Docker container on a laptop or in the cloud.

themes.theme: Flaky test elimination as AI agent reliability prerequisite

insights.claim: Flaky tests that produce non-deterministic pass/fail results are disproportionately harmful in AI agent workflows because they prevent agents from receiving a clean signal on code quality, forcing expensive manual log review that halts autonomous operation.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Flaky test detection and remediation tooling becomes critical infrastructure for any organization running continuous AI coding agents; it is a prerequisite, not an optimization.
insights.quote: As soon as you delve into, was it a flaky test, or let me go look at the logs...you're back to having to manually dig through everything, and everything grinds to a halt.

insights.claim: CircleCI built an AI-powered flaky test fixer that initially failed due to hallucinations (the model claimed to run tests it never actually ran), then succeeded by shifting from an open-ended agent to a predefined workflow loop that uses CI pipelines as the ground-truth judge.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: The 'CI as judge' pattern—using existing test infrastructure rather than LLM self-assessment to validate agent output—is a proven reliability pattern for production AI coding workflows.
insights.quote: Instead of LLM-as-a-judge or even trying to get the LLM to rerun the test, we thought we had a fix. Let's go run it in CI, and at the very least we can verify that there's no regressions.

insights.claim: CircleCI uses test result history (test name, git SHA, pass/fail outcome, reruns) already stored in its platform database to infer flakiness, then triggers an AI agent loop to diagnose and fix each flaky test—demonstrating that CI platforms with historical test data have a structural data advantage for AI-powered quality tooling.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: CI platforms with rich historical test result data are uniquely positioned to build AI quality products; this is a moat that pure code-gen tools cannot easily replicate.
insights.quote: It turned out that we actually had all the data that we needed to do this already. One of the things that our platform does is we record your test results.

themes.theme: AI coding success patterns and organizational prerequisites

insights.claim: AI mechanical code transformations work reliably when senior engineers have already invested in well-structured repositories with clear patterns and rules—the prerequisite is structured repositories, not model capability.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Engineering orgs that invested in clean architecture and strong conventions before AI adoption will see disproportionately better AI coding ROI; tech debt is an AI productivity multiplier in reverse.
insights.quote: The models, especially when you're running on a laptop with full project context, they're able to produce code that suits our most senior engineer's standards, largely because those folks have invested in making sure their repositories are structured in a way that is easy to follow.

insights.claim: CircleCI's design team—not developers—led a dark mode implementation across the entire UI by providing domain expertise on visual design while AI agents performed all mechanical component transformations, demonstrating that AI enables non-developer contributions to previously deprioritized technical work.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: AI coding tools expand the contributor pool beyond developers, enabling domain experts (designers, product managers) to drive technical projects that would never be prioritized by engineering teams alone.
insights.quote: Our design team led this. They had a lot of domain expertise on how they wanted the UI to look...they were able to go and create all of the stylings, and then let the agents largely do most of the mechanical transformation work.

insights.claim: Results from AI coding tools remain 'highly variable' even within single organizations, varying by team, with quality heavily dependent on the existing quality of codebase patterns—suggesting AI is an amplifier of existing engineering quality, not a leveler.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Enterprise software vendors selling AI coding tools to organizations with poor code hygiene may face high churn; customer success and onboarding around codebase preparation could be a key differentiator.
insights.quote: Results are still highly variable. Justin from DX talked about this like, between companies, there's a lot of variation. I found that even within our organization, there's variation between teams.

themes.theme: SDLC linear model obsolescence and future delivery architecture

insights.claim: Webster argues the sequential build-test-deploy mental model of software delivery is fundamentally incompatible with AI agent throughput; parallelizing a system through a serialized pipeline creates permanent bottlenecks regardless of per-stage optimization.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: The 'CI as serial gate' paradigm will be disrupted; next-generation delivery platforms built around parallel, asynchronous validation rather than sequential pipelines will capture the agentic-era market.
insights.quote: The worst thing about having a parallel system that's providing work is forcing everything through a serialized pipeline. That will just never work.

insights.claim: Webster predicts the future delivery model involves asynchronous, parallel validation events that are later combined into a single deployment gate—eliminating the requirement that PR review or code review must complete before deployment, which he frames as an artifact of distrust in test suites rather than a fundamental requirement.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: Continuous deployment platforms that can attest to test outcomes independently of linear PR workflows will be architecturally favored in agentic-native engineering organizations.
insights.quote: I think this model of breaking down these very linear processes...and turning it into something where things are happening at various points in the process across time...what more do you need? For continuous delivery, if the tests pass, you should deploy the code.

insights.claim: CI/CD pipelines are evolving from developer convenience infrastructure into mission-critical business infrastructure as organizations run AI coding agents 24/7, making pipeline reliability (uptime, flakiness) a revenue-impacting concern rather than a developer experience issue.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: CI/CD platform SLAs and reliability products will command premium pricing as they become critical-path infrastructure; this elevates the strategic and financial importance of platforms like CircleCI.
insights.quote: Many companies want to run agents 24/7. They want to churn tokens. They want to have AI teammates that are able to work. The pipelines that we build aren't just like, guess who gets to play foosball today if CI is down?

themes.theme: CircleCI Chunk product and competitive positioning

insights.claim: CircleCI is building an agent called 'Chunk' that packages its internal learnings—test impact analysis, flaky test fixes, AI code review—into a validation-first product designed to keep software production-ready as AI agents continuously generate code.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: CircleCI is transitioning from pure CI/CD infrastructure to an AI quality and delivery intelligence platform; this product direction reflects a broader market opportunity for validation-first AI tooling.
insights.quote: One of the things that we're working on is this agent we're calling Chunk. The idea of Chunk is to really take all of these learnings that we've had internally.

insights.claim: CircleCI's competitive moat in the agentic era is historical CI data—knowing when builds break, when merges happen, and when rollbacks occur gives it a unique dataset to train and improve AI quality agents over time.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: CI platforms with long customer histories and rich build/test telemetry have structural data advantages for AI-powered quality products that newer entrants cannot quickly replicate.
insights.quote: One of the nice things about a CI system is it knows when you break things. We know when you merge. We know when you revert. We know when you roll back.

insights.claim: Webster acknowledges that AI code review via internal review bots has shown success for developers working in unfamiliar codebases by applying deterministic rule-based feedback, but is still an investment area rather than a solved problem at CircleCI.
insights.speaker: Michael Webster
insights.so_what: AI-assisted code review is a growing product category but still maturing; vendors offering deterministic rule enforcement (style, security, design patterns) may have more defensible positioning than pure LLM review tools.
insights.quote: If you're able to encode your rules, apply those in a review in a deterministic fashion, so whatever rules you have, for loop, apply them, that's really useful for someone who is unfamiliar with the codebase.
